Our office, laboratory, and warehouse are located in Barcelona, Spain, where we test and curate new fragrances, assemble and package orders, and manage dispatches across Europe. From here, we ship both small and large domestic and international orders. Yes, we work with clients beyond the EU, supporting makers and brands worldwide. Large orders requiring additional documentation are processed and shipped without delay — we consider it a core commitment.

We work closely with leading suppliers of fragrance oils, waxes, containers, and wicks from France, Germany, Italy, Switzerland, and the USA. pages.title: Wick Sizing Chart for Aroma and Wax TCR and Stabilo Series Wicks
headings: Wick Sizing Chart for Aroma and Wax TCR and Stabilo Series Wicks
textBlocks: This wick sizing chart provides starting recommendations for pairing Aroma and Wax’s TCR Series and Stabilo Series cotton wicks with the following waxes: Golden Wax™ 464 Container Soy Wax, NatureWax™ C-3 Container Soy Wax, EcoSoya™ CB-Advanced Container Wax, EcoSoya™ PillarBlend Wax, KeraSoy™ Pillar Wax 4120, and EcoOlive™ Pillar Wax. Recommendations are based on container or mold diameter in millimeters. Always conduct burn tests to account for fragrance load (6–10%), dyes, and specific wax blends, as these factors affect wick performance. Visit aromawax.eu for product details and additional candle-making guides.

TCR Series Wicks: Flat-braided cotton-and-paper wicks with an outer jacket, ideal for vegetable waxes like soy, rapeseed, and olive-based waxes. They minimize soot and mushrooming, offering versatility for container and pillar candles.

Stabilo Series Wicks: Coreless, flat-braided cotton wicks with paper filaments, suited for soy and harder-to-melt waxes like KeraSoy Pillar 4120. They promote consistent capillary action and reduce carbon buildup, especially in high-fragrance candles.
Golden Wax™ 464 Container Soy Wax: Creamy, low-melt-point soy wax with good fragrance throw; requires larger wicks to avoid tunneling.
NatureWax™ C-3 Container Soy Wax: Smooth, natural soy wax with excellent glass adhesion; may need slight wicking up for deeper melt pools.
EcoSoya™ CB-Advanced Container Wax: Advanced soy wax with strong scent throw and frosting resistance; smaller wicks suffice due to efficient burn.
EcoSoya™ PillarBlend Wax: Soy-based wax for pillars and votives; larger wicks ensure mold release and full melt pools.
KeraSoy™ Pillar Wax 4120: Vegan-friendly soy blend for pillars; formulated for single pours, requires larger wicks for high fragrance loads.
EcoOlive™ Pillar Wax: Dense, olive-based wax for pillars; larger wicks or multi-wicking needed for wider molds to achieve full melt.
Burn candles for 4 hours to assess melt pool (aim for 5–10 mm depth, reaching container edges).
Test three wick sizes (e.g., TCR 27/16, 30/18, 33/18) to determine the optimal fit.
Adjust for fragrance loads above 8% or heavy dyes, which may clog wicks and require larger sizes.
For pillar candles, ensure wicks support mold release without cracking; test after 48 hours of cooling.
For diameters over 75 mm, consider multiple wicks (e.g., 2x TCR 27/16 for 100 mm).
Calculate single-wick size by dividing diameter by number of wicks (e.g., 100 mm ÷ 2 = 50 mm).
Space wicks evenly (approximately 30–40 mm apart) to ensure uniform burning.
High fragrance loads (8–10%) or dark dyes may require wicking up to maintain flame stability.
Use fragrance oils compatible with natural waxes (IFRA-certified, available at aromawax.eu).
Center wicks using wick bars or holders (available at aromawax.eu).
Trim wicks to 5–7 mm before each burn to minimize soot and mushrooming.
For KeraSoy Pillar 4120, avoid heating above 85°C to prevent discoloration.
Measure the inner diameter of the container or mold in millimeters.
Identify the wax type from aromawax.eu/collections/waxes.
Select the recommended TCR or Stabilo wick as a starting point.
Add 6–10% fragrance and any dyes, then conduct burn tests for 4 hours.
Observe melt pool, flame size, and burn behavior (e.g., tunneling, soot, mushrooming).
Adjust wick size up or down based on results, repeating tests until optimal performance is achieved.
Container Candles: Golden Wax 464, NatureWax C-3, and EcoSoya CB-Advanced adhere well to glass; ensure wicks create a full melt pool without overheating.
Pillar Candles: EcoSoya PillarBlend, KeraSoy 4120, and EcoOlive Pillar shrink slightly for easy mold release; use larger wicks to support structure.
Burn Testing: Allow candles to cool for 48 hours before testing to ensure accurate burn behavior.
Wick Compatibility: TCR wicks excel with vegetable waxes like EcoOlive and KeraSoy, while Stabilo wicks handle high-fragrance soy blends effectively.
Tunneling: Wick too small; try a larger size (e.g., TCR 30/18 instead of 27/16).
Soot/Mushrooming: Wick too large or incompatible fragrance; wick down or switch to Stabilo.
Shallow Melt Pool: Increase wick size or use multiple wicks for wider containers.

This chart serves as a starting point. Variations in wax blends, fragrance composition, dye concentration, and container/mold shape may require adjustments. For detailed wick descriptions, wax specifications, and candle-making resources, visit aromawax.eu.

blogs.title: How to Choose the Best Wax for Candle Making | Wax Guide – AROMA + WAX
blogs.metaDescription: Discover how to choose the perfect wax with a clear comparison of soy, beeswax, paraffin and coconut blends by burn, scent throw and practical use.
headings: How to Choose Wax
Best Candle Waxes Compared: Soy vs Coconut vs Paraffin
Room Spray Guide: Ratios, Bases, and Fragrance Oils
textBlocks: Picking the right wax can make or break a candle project. It decides how long the candle burns, how strong the fragrance smells, and even how it looks on a shelf. With many options available, a candle maker should understand each wax’s properties to match the wax type to their project’s goals and values. Key factors include the kind of candle (container, pillar, etc.), whether the wax is natural or synthetic, the desired aesthetics, and the needed burn qualities. This guide breaks down the basics of wax types, helping beginners and hobbyists create candles that shine.

The candle-making world offers a variety of waxes, each with distinct characteristics. Natural waxes like soy, beeswax, and coconut are popular for their eco-friendly profiles, while synthetic options like paraffin remain widely used for their affordability in large-scale production, such as creating scented candles for events, balancing cost and performance effectively. Blended waxes, such as soy-coconut or paraffin-soy mixes, are also gaining traction for combining the best traits of multiple waxes. Some waxes excel in fragrance retention, others in burn time or sustainability. Knowing these options allows candle makers to align their choices with specific goals, whether prioritizing scent, aesthetics, or environmental responsibility.
Natural and synthetic waxes cater to different needs in candle making. Here’s a breakdown of their differences:
Natural waxes, such as soy, beeswax, and coconut, originate from plants or insects, offering renewable resources and reduced environmental impact with cleaner combustion.
Synthetic paraffin wax, petroleum-based, provides consistent texture and robust fragrance throw but may produce more soot during burning.

Soy wax, extracted from soybean oil, is a popular natural wax in candle making. It’s renewable and sustainable, making it popular for eco-conscious users. Soy wax offers a long burn time, excellent fragrance retention (up to 10% fragrance load), and a creamy texture ideal for container candles. It can sometimes produce uneven tops and may be sensitive to temperature changes.
Burn Example: A typical 200g soy candle burns approximately 40–50 hours.
Pillar candles if blended with other waxes

Beeswax is another natural option. It burns cleanly with very little smoke or soot, giving candles a mild, pleasant honey scent. Beeswax candles have a long burn time and a beautiful natural color. But beeswax is typically more expensive than other waxes, making it less suitable for large-scale production. Its limited fragrance retention makes it less ideal for heavily scented candles. Beeswax excels in applications prioritizing air quality, such as meditation or yoga spaces, where its natural properties support a pure environment, appealing to premium markets.
Burn Example: A typical 200g beeswax candle burns approximately 50–60 hours.

Paraffin wax is a synthetic wax derived from petroleum. It’s affordable, versatile, and easy to work with, making it a popular choice for beginners and large-scale candle makers. Paraffin wax candles have excellent scent throw and smooth appearance. The downside is that paraffin wax can produce more soot and is less eco-friendly.
Burn Example: A typical 200g paraffin candle burns approximately 35–45 hours.
Paraffin wax is best used for:

Coconut wax, derived from coconut oil, emerges as a premium natural wax with a glossy creamy finish and clean burn. Pure coconut wax is soft and expensive, so it’s usually used in blends. It blends effectively with soy or beeswax for stability, poured at 54-66°C. Soy-coconut blends combine soy’s longevity with coconut’s luxury, ideal for high-end products like mango-scented tin candles for spa gifts.
Burn Example: A typical 200g coconut wax candle burns approximately 45–55 hours.

When selecting wax, consider fragrance load—how much scent the wax can hold without issues. Waxes like paraffin are known for their superior hot scent throw, making them ideal for strong fragrances, while soy and beeswax provide a more subtle aroma that suits those seeking natural scents or gentler fragrance notes. Coconut wax blends combine excellent fragrance performance with luxury aesthetics, perfect for high-end candles.

Burn time is another important factor; natural options like beeswax and soy burn slower, making them cost-effective for longer durations. On the other hand, paraffin wax may burn faster but delivers a bright, intense flame. Additives like vybar (1-2%) harden wax, extending burn time, and stearic acid (3-5%) enhances fragrance release, improving overall performance based on project needs. Factors to consider include:
Appearance matters, too. Soy and coconut yield smooth, creamy tops; paraffin provides glossy finishes.

Sustainability plays a significant role in wax selection for candle making. Natural waxes, including soy and beeswax, are renewable and biodegradable, supporting agricultural and beekeeping industries. Soy wax reduces carbon footprints, while beeswax aids pollinator health, aligning with eco-friendly goals. Paraffin wax, petroleum-based, offers less sustainable options with higher emissions. Opting for a soy-coconut blend promotes a green profile, appealing to customers seeking sustainable candle products.

Matching the wax type to your candle-making goals involves balancing cost, burn quality, fragrance load, appearance, and sustainability. Understanding these factors helps you create candles that meet your specific needs effectively and efficiently. Choose your wax thoughtfully, considering each wax’s unique advantages and limitations to achieve the best candle-making results.

textBlocks: Candle making transforms simple materials into glowing creations that light up homes and hearts. This DIY craft offers a creative outlet, perfect for crafting homemade gifts or building a small business. Candles bring warmth and ambiance, and mastering this skill allows anyone to customize scents, colors, and shapes. This guide provides a step-by-step path to create stunning candles while ensuring safety and quality.

Crafting candles requires specific tools and materials to achieve professional results. A well-prepared workspace streamlines the process and ensures safety. Key items include a double boiler for melting wax, a thermometer to monitor temperatures, and wicks suited to container sizes. Fragrance oils and dyes add personality, while molds or containers shape the final product.
Double boiler: Melts wax evenly without burning.
Thermometer: Ensures wax is at the correct temperature (e.g., 85°C for pouring).
Wicks: Choose cotton or wooden, sized for candle diameter.
Fragrance oils: They produce a unique long-lasting scent.
Containers or molds: Glass jars or silicone molds for versatility.
Pouring pitcher: For precise wax pouring.
These materials form the foundation of candle making, enabling beginners to start with confidence.
Different waxes suit specific candle types, from container candles to pillars. Each wax has unique properties, affecting burn time, scent throw, and appearance.
Soy wax: Derived from soybeans, eco-friendly, with a density of 0.88. Melts at 45–50°C, ideal for containers. Holds 6–10% fragrance, requiring 7–14 days curing. Perfect for smooth, creamy finishes.
Rapeseed wax: Plant-based, density of 0.89, melts at 40–50°C. Suits containers and pillars, holds 6–10% fragrance. Cures in 5–10 days, offering strong scent throw and matte textures.
Paraffin wax: Petroleum-based, density of 0.87, melts at 50–60°C. Versatile for all candle types, holds 6–12% fragrance. Cures in 3–7 days, ensuring vibrant colors and glossy finishes.
Olive wax: A blend of olive and soy, density of 0.88, melts at 50–55°C. Best for containers, holds 8–10% fragrance. Cures in 7 days, delivering smooth surfaces and excellent scent retention.
Choosing wax depends on the desired candle type and aesthetic.

Fragrance oils define a candle’s character, creating a memorable scent throw—how far the aroma spreads when lit or unlit. Cold throw (unlit) and hot throw (lit) depend on wax type and fragrance quality. High-quality oils ensure a balanced scent that lasts. Add fragrance at 70–85°C to preserve aroma compounds, using 6–10% for most waxes.
Why does this matter? Proper fragrance integration prevents weak scents or oil seepage. Test oils with small batches to confirm compatibility with wax.

Coloring candles adds visual appeal, but safety is key. Use dye chips or liquid dyes formulated for candle making, as regular food coloring can disrupt burning. Add dyes at 80–85°C for even distribution, starting with small amounts (0.1–0.5% of wax weight). Test colors on a small batch to avoid overpowering the wax or affecting burn quality.

Why is this important? Safe dyes prevent wick clogging and ensure clean burns. Pastel shades suit delicate scents, while bold hues enhance dramatic fragrances. Stir thoroughly to avoid streaks, creating a polished, professional look for homemade candles.
This step-by-step guide simplifies the process, ensuring your initial foray into candle making is a success.

Prepare Your Workspace: Cover your work surface to protect it from spills. Ensure you have all your materials and tools ready:wax, wicks,fragranceoil, dye (if using), pouring pot, double boiler, thermometer, stirring utensil, and chosen containers. Safety first: keep flammable items away and ensure good ventilation.

Measure and Melt the Wax: Weigh the required amount of wax using a scale. Place the wax in the pouring pot and melt it gently using a double boiler method. Stir occasionally until the wax is completely liquid. Use a thermometer to monitor the temperature according to the specific wax type’s instructions. Avoid overheating the wax.

Prepare the Wicks and Containers: While the wax is melting, secure the wicks to the bottom center of your candle containers. You can use a wick sticker or a dab of melted wax. Use a wick holder or two craft sticks placed across the top of the container to keep the wick straight and centered.

Add Fragrance and Color: Once the wax reaches the recommended temperature for adding fragrance (refer to wax and fragrance oil instructions), remove it from the heat. Gently stir in the measuredfragranceoil for at least two minutes to ensure thorough mixing. If using liquid or block dye, add it at this stage until the desired color is achieved.

Pour the Wax: Check the wax temperature again; it should be at the correct pouring temperature for your specific wax. Carefully pour the melted and scented wax into the prepared containers, avoiding the wick holder. Fill to the desired level, leaving some space at the top.

Cool and Cure: Allow the candles to cool completely at room temperature, undisturbed, for several hours or overnight. Avoid rapid cooling as it can cause cracks or sinkholes. Once fully cooled, trim the wick to about 1/4 inch before the first burn. For best results, allow your home made candles to cure for a few days to a week, depending on the wax and fragrance used, to enhance the scent throw. This curing time allows the fragrance to fully bind with the wax.
This process ensures a professional candle with a strong scent and clean burn.

Mistakes in candle making can lead to poor burns or weak scents. Avoid these pitfalls for better results. Overheating wax (above 90°C) degrades fragrance, while pouring too hot (above 70°C) causes sinkholes. Use a thermometer to monitor temperatures. Incorrect wick sizes lead to tunneling or sooting—choose wicks based on container diameter (e.g., 3–4 cm wicks for 200 ml jars).

Why does this matter? Precise techniques save materials and improve candle quality. Test small batches to perfect fragrance and color, and cure candles properly to enhance scent throw. These steps elevate homemade candles to professional standards.

Safety is critical in candle making to prevent burns, fires, or spills. Always work in a well-ventilated area, away from flammable items. Use heat-resistant gloves when handling hot wax, and never leave melting wax unattended. Store fragrance oils and dyes in cool, dry places to maintain quality. Following safety rules prevents accidents and preserves the joy of crafting candles.

textBlocks: Container candles bring warmth and charm to any space, and making them at home is rewarding. This guide offers a step-by-step approach to crafting container candles with wax, wicks, and fragrance. Perfect for beginners, it covers supplies, techniques, and tips to ensure success. Learn why each step matters and create custom candles that glow beautifully.

Container candles are safe, and simple to make. Housed in jars or bottles, they reduce mess and fire risks compared to pillar candles. Making your own allows control over wax types, fragrance, and design, saving money and sparking creativity. Whether for gifts or home decor, crafting container candles is a practical, fun hobby that delivers stunning results.
Quality supplies are essential for making container candles. Having the right tools ensures safety and professional outcomes. Here’s what’s needed:
Wax (soy wax, paraffin, olive blend, or coconut-soy) – 0.5–1 kg.
Scale for measuring wax and fragrance.
These items lay the groundwork for successful candle making.

The container defines the candle’s look and burn quality. Glass jars, bottles, or metal tins are ideal, but they must be heat-resistant. A 0.2 kg container holds about 0.16 kg of wax. Wide-mouth containers prevent tunneling (uneven burning). Ensure containers are clean and dry before use to avoid wax adhesion issues. Selecting the right container enhances safety and elevates the candle’s aesthetic appeal.
Wax choice affects burn time, fragrance throw, and eco-friendliness. Here are the top options for container candles:
Soy Wax: Natural, burns 25-30 hours per 100 g. Creamy texture, eco-friendly, but may frost (white patches). Pour at 55–75°C for smooth adhesion. Ideal for sustainable candles.
Paraffin Wax: Synthetic, burns 13-17 hours per 100 g. Affordable, with strong fragrance and color retention. Less sustainable, may produce soot. Pour at 49–71°C.
Coconut-Soy Blend: Natural, burns 27–38 hours per 100 g. Smooth, frost-resistant finish with excellent scent throw. Pour at 38–46°C for glossy results.

Olive Blend Wax: A natural blend incorporating olive oil, burns 17-25 hours per 100 g. Offers a silky texture and good fragrance hold, similar to soy wax, with a subtle earthy note. Eco-friendly and renewable, it’s softer, so best for containers. Pour at 65–70°C to avoid cracking. Perfect for artisanal, green-focused candles.
Soy wax and olive blends suit eco-conscious projects, while paraffin is budget-friendly. Coconut-soy blends balance performance and aesthetics.

The wick controls the candle’s burn. A wick too small causes tunneling; too large creates soot. Match wick size to container diameter (e.g., 5 cm container needs a medium wick). Cotton wicks work well with soy wax and olive blends, while wooden wicks suit both natural waxes and paraffin. Secure wicks to the container’s base with stickers or glue. Proper wick selection maximizes burn time and prevents issues.

Fragrance and color customize container candles. Use 6–10% fragrance oil (e.g., 10 g per 100 g wax) for a balanced scent. Add at 65–74°C to bind well with wax, stirring gently for 2 minutes. Olive blend wax holds fragrance similarly to soy wax, enhancing earthy or herbal scents. For color, use liquid or chip dyes sparingly to avoid clogging the wick. Test batches to perfect scent and hue. Proper additions enhance appeal without affecting performance.
Follow these steps to craft container candles:
Melt wax in a double boiler to 82–93°C.
Cool wax to 55–75°C, then add fragrance and dye.
Secure wick to container’s base with a sticker.
Pour wax slowly at 55–75°C (soy wax) or 65–70°C (olive blend).
Center wick using a clip or stick.
Pouring at the right temperature ensures a smooth surface and strong container adhesion, especially for softer waxes like olive blends.

Let candles cool undisturbed for 24–48 hours after pouring. Soy wax and olive blend candles require 7–14 days to cure, enhancing fragrance throw. Paraffin cures in 3–5 days. Trim the wick to 0.6 cm before burning to reduce soot and ensure a steady flame. Proper cooling and curing improve scent and burn quality, while wick trimming promotes safety and extends candle life.
Avoid these common errors when making container candles:
Tunneling: Choose the correct wick size for the container.
Wet Spots: Pour at the right temperature (e.g., 55–75°C for olive blend).
Weak Scent: Increase fragrance load or add at 65–70°C.
Cracked Surfaces: Cool slowly, avoiding drafts.
Testing small batches with waxes like olive blend or soy wax helps refine techniques and ensures consistent, high-quality candles.

Safety is paramount in candle making. Work in a ventilated area, away from flammable materials. Never leave melting wax unattended. Use a thermometer to avoid overheating (above 93°C). When burning, place candles on flat, heat-resistant surfaces, away from drafts. Trim wicks to 0.6 cm and never leave burning candles unattended. These precautions ensure safe crafting and use, protecting users and enhancing enjoyment.

Making container candles is a creative, fulfilling process. By selecting the right wax — whether soy wax, olive blend, or paraffin — and pairing it with the perfect wick and container, anyone can craft stunning candles. Experiment with fragrances and colors to personalize your creations. With practice, container candles become unique works of art that light up any space with warmth and style.

textBlocks: Choosing the right wick is critical for crafting high-quality candles that burn cleanly and evenly. Premium wicks like TCR and STABILO are offered to ensure optimal performance. This guide explores the importance of wick size, factors influencing wick choice, and practical testing methods to achieve perfect results in candle making. Whether creating soy wax candles or experimenting with blends, understanding wick selection enhances the candle-making process.

Using an incorrect wick size disrupts candle performance. A wick that’s too small causes tunneling, where wax burns unevenly, leaving walls of unburned wax. Conversely, a wick too large produces excessive soot, mushrooming, or an overly large flame, compromising safety and aesthetics. Proper wick selection ensures a stable burn pool, maximizing fragrance throw and candle longevity. Selecting the right wick from prevents these issues, delivering professional results.

Several factors determine the ideal wick for a candle. The wax type, container size, fragrance load, and dyes all play roles. Soy wax, for instance, requires a different wick than paraffin due to its viscosity. Container diameter dictates burn pool size, influencing wick choice. Fragrance and dyes can alter burn characteristics, requiring adjustments. Understanding these variables helps candle makers select wicks that ensure clean, efficient burns for any project.
Wax type significantly impacts wick selection. Here’s how different waxes interact with wicks:
Soy Wax: Burns cooler, often requiring larger wicks to achieve a full melt pool.
Paraffin Wax: Burns hotter, typically needing smaller wicks to control flame size.
Beeswax: Dense and slow-burning, often paired with thicker wicks.
Coconut Wax: Soft and creamy, requiring wicks that balance its low melting point.
Choosing wicks compatible with the wax type ensures optimal burn performance. Aromawax.eu offers wicks tailored for various waxes, simplifying the selection process.
Proper wick sizing enhances wax performance, preventing issues like uneven burning or weak fragrance throw. Testing wicks with specific waxes is essential for consistent results.

Container diameter directly affects wick size. A narrow container (e.g., 50 mm) needs a smaller wick to avoid overheating, while a wider container (e.g., 100 mm) requires a larger or multiple wicks for a full melt pool. Mismatched wicks lead to tunneling or excessive soot. Measuring the container’s inner diameter before selecting a wick ensures compatibility. Aromawax.eu provides wick sizing charts to match different types of wicks with various container sizes, streamlining the candle-making process.

Fragrance oils and dyes alter how a candle burns, affecting wick choice. Heavy fragrance loads (8–10%) can clog wicks, requiring larger sizes to maintain a stable flame. Dyes, especially dark colors, increase wax viscosity, impacting burn behavior. Testing wicks with specific fragrance and dye combinations is crucial for optimal performance. High-quality fragrance oils pair well with TCR wicks, ensuring strong scent throw without compromising burn quality.
Different wick series cater to specific candle-making needs. Key options include:
STABILO Cotton Wicks: Known for consistent burn and minimal mushrooming, ideal for soy wax.
TCR Cotton Wicks: Versatile, suitable for various waxes, offering excellent fragrance throw.
ECO Cotton Wicks: Designed for natural waxes, reducing soot and enhancing eco-friendliness.
Wooden Wicks: Create a crackling effect, best for wide containers, pillar candles and aesthetic appeal.

Each series addresses unique requirements, from wax compatibility to burn aesthetics. Selecting the appropriate wick series ensures candles meet performance and aesthetic goals, enhancing the overall experience.
Testing wicks is essential to find the perfect match. Follow these steps:

Prepare a test candle with the chosen wax, fragrance, and container. Select three wick sizes (small, medium, large) for comparison. Make candles with each size and, when your candles are ready to be used, burn each candle for 4 hours, observing melt pool and flame size. Check for tunneling, soot, or mushrooming after cooling. Adjust wick size based on results, repeating until optimal.
Prepare a test candle with the chosen wax, fragrance, and container.
Select three wick sizes (small, medium, large) for comparison.
Make candles with each size and, when your candles are ready to be used, burn each candle for 4 hours, observing melt pool and flame size.
Check for tunneling, soot, or mushrooming after cooling.
Adjust wick size based on results, repeating until optimal.
This method ensures accurate wick sizing, saving time and materials.
Common wick-related issues can be resolved with proper adjustments.
Tunneling occurs when the wick is too small, preventing a full melt pool. Increase wick size to fix this.
Mushrooming, where the wick tip forms a carbon ball, indicates a wick too large or incompatible with the wax-fragrance mix; try a smaller wick or STABILO series.
Soot results from oversized wicks or heavy dyes; reduce wick size or use ECO or TCR wicks.

Selecting the right wick transforms candle-making outcomes, ensuring clean burns, strong fragrance throw, and aesthetic appeal. By considering wax type, container size, fragrance load, and wick series like TCR or STABILO, candle makers can achieve professional results. Testing and troubleshooting further refine the process.

textBlocks: Selecting the right base is essential when crafting reed diffusers or room sprays, significantly influencing fragrance dispersion, product performance, and customer satisfaction. This guide provides clear instructions to help you make the optimal choice for your specific project.

A base is a liquid used to dilute fragrance oils, facilitating effective scent diffusion and ensuring product safety. Choosing the correct base directly affects how well your fragrance travels and lasts.
For Reed Diffusers: Select a base with low viscosity to ensure fragrance oils travel smoothly up the reeds, resulting in even and steady evaporation.
For Room Sprays: Opt for a base that evaporates quickly, leaving no residue on surfaces or fabrics.

Pros: сost-effective, easily accessible, good solvent properties. Cons: higher viscosity; can hinder reed diffusion, risk of reed clogging, possible residue and oily finish. Best for: Budget-sensitive projects with less stringent diffusion requirements.

Pros: rapid evaporation, strong initial fragrance impact, excellent for room sprays. Cons: highly flammable, noticeable alcohol smell, careful handling, may need solubilizers (e.g., polysorbate, PEG-40) for proper fragrance blending. Best for: Room sprays requiring quick drying and impactful fragrance bursts.
3. VOC Base, e.g. Augeo Clean Multi

Pros: low odor; doesn’t compete with fragrance, environmentally friendly, bio-based, biodegradable, efficient diffusion in both reed diffusers and room sprays, no additional solubilizers needed. Cons: slightly more expensive. Best for: Premium-quality products, environmentally-conscious brands, or versatile production needs.
Consider the following detailed steps to determine the ideal base:

Reed Diffusers: Ensure the base has a low viscosity (thin consistency) for optimal reed absorption and steady evaporation. Room Sprays: Choose a base designed for quick evaporation to avoid residue or stains. Multi-Product Use: Selecting a versatile base simplifies production for multiple product lines.
Step 2: Assess Compatibility with Your Fragrance Oils
Check how well your fragrance oils blend with your chosen base.
Alcohol-based bases often require additional solubilizers; non-alcoholic bases like Augeo Clean Multi usually blend well on their own.
Step 3: Determine the Desired Customer Experience
For a safer and cleaner home fragrance, select bases with minimal volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Decide if a strong initial scent or a long-lasting, subtle fragrance is your priority.

Traditional bases like DPM typically contain petroleum derivatives. You might want to choose eco-friendly alternatives for a sustainable and safe formulation. Always follow labeling regulations, especially for flammable or allergenic ingredients.
Testing: Always conduct small-scale trials first to evaluate scent throw, evaporation rates, and potential residue.
Reed Diffusers: Approximately 70–80% base to 20–30% fragrance oil.
Room Sprays: Typically around 10–20% fragrance oil, following IFRA guidelines.
Safety and Labeling: Clearly label your products with safety information, usage instructions, and ingredient disclosures.

textBlocks: Choosing the right vessel is key to making effective and elegant reed diffusers or room sprays. Whether you're creating home fragrance products to sell or just for yourself, the container you use affects how the product looks, performs, and even smells. In this guide, we’ll break down how to choose the best bottles for reed diffusers and room sprays — and help you get the most out of your fragrance oils, reeds, and bases.
When selecting a bottle for your reed diffuser, consider:
Neck Size: A bottle with a narrow neck slows down evaporation and helps the scent last longer. Wide-neck bottles let the base evaporate too quickly.
Material: Glass is ideal — it’s non-reactive and shows off your fragrance blend beautifully.
Volume: Most reed diffuser bottles range from 50ml to 200ml. The bigger the bottle, the longer it will last.
Browse our reed diffuser bottles here.
Reeds are what carry the fragrance from the liquid into the air. You have two main choices:
Rattan reeds: Natural and classic. Look for ones with open channels for best scent throw.
Fibre reeds: Great for strong or consistent scent release and available in various colours.
Keep in mind: more reeds = stronger scent. But using too many in a small space may overwhelm the room.
See our full selection of diffuser reeds here.
Most reed diffusers aren’t made with fragrance oil alone — you’ll need a base to thin the oil and help it travel up the reeds.
Read our bases guide to pick the right base.
Room sprays are a fast and easy way to freshen up a space. They’re simple to make, but the right bottle helps you get the best results.
Room spray bottles need to work well with your chosen formula. Look for:
Spray performance: Fine mist is ideal for even fragrance distribution.
Material: Glass or PET plastic are most common. Make sure it’s safe for use with alcohol or water-based mixes.
Size: Common sizes are 50 ml to 100 ml, great for personal or retail use.
Find room spray bottles in our collection here.
If you’re using strong oils or high fragrance loads, make sure the spray mechanism can handle it without clogging. Test your formula in small batches first.

Whether you're making a diffuser or a spray, your fragrance oil is the star. Choose oils that are designed for home fragrance products — and test your scent in both formats to make sure it performs the way you want.
Explore all our fragrance oils for diffusers and sprays here.

Always test your full setup (bottle + reeds or sprayer + fragrance) before launching or gifting. Don’t overfill bottles — leave room at the top, especially for room sprays. Use caps and plugs to seal reed diffusers if selling or transporting them.

textBlocks: When you decide to create your very first candle collection, it’s an exciting step into building your brand. In this article, we won’t talk about the budget or costs of such a project — that part will always depend on your choices and priorities. Instead, we’ll guide you through the most important aspects you need to focus on when building your collection.
Before you start choosing fragrances, jars, or waxes, it’s crucial to ask yourself: Why am I creating this collection?
Your goal will shape all your next decisions. For example:
To test the market→ keep it simple, with a small number of scents and standard jars.
To launch a seasonal edition → focus on trendy fragrances and colors tied to holidays or the time of year.
To establish your brand identity → build a signature style with unique packaging and a cohesive theme.
To offer gifts → think about presentation, ready-made boxes, and versatile scents that appeal to a wide audience.

Once you define your goal, you’ll have a clear foundation for deciding on the fragrances, containers, waxes, and packaging that make the most sense for your collection. The real challenge is not just to make candles — it’s to create a collection that feels cohesive, memorable, and true to your vision.
Fragrance is at the heart of every candle. When creating a collection you plan to sell, consider:

Seasonality: Fresh citrus or floral notes work beautifully in spring/summer, while warm spices and woods are perfect for autumn/winter. Do not forget about holidays and special occasions - they require special scents: Christmas calls for gourmand and Christmas tree fragrancies, weddings and celebrations pair with blossoming flowers and champagne scents.

Trends: Keep an eye on trending scents — for example, gourmand notes like vanilla and coconut, or earthy tones such as matcha and pepper. Trends change and you can use it in your collection - watch the perfumers share their plans for an upcoming season.
Local preferences: Certain regions lean towards particular styles — lighter fragrances in warmer climates, cozy scents in colder areas.

Balance: A collection usually works best when it includes a variety — something fresh, something warm, something bold. Make sure, your scents differ from each other to satisfy demand and tastes of a wider audience.
Explore our curated selection of fragrance oils to inspire your blend.
The look and feel of your containers will define the first impression of your collection. Think about:
Trends: Matte finishes and earthy tones are currently very popular.
Target audience: Minimalist designs appeal to younger buyers, while luxurious jars may be more suited for premium collections.
Seasonality: Amber or dark jars for winter, lighter clear or white jars for summer.
Practicality: Ensure containers are heat-safe and the right size for your chosen wick and wax.
Discover our range of jars and lids to match your collection’s identity.
Your wax choice affects both performance and sustainability. Consider:
Soy waxes (like Golden Wax 464 or NatureWax C-3) are customer favorites for container candles.
Blended waxes such as EcoSoya CB-Advanced offer a smoother finish and great fragrance throw.

Pillar waxes are ideal if you plan to expand your collection into wax melts, votives, or sculptural candles. From our range you can try: EcoSoya™ PillarBlend Wax, KeraSoy™ Pillar Wax 4120, EcoOlive™ Pillar Wax.

The wick is a crucial detail — it ensures your candles burn safely and evenly. Wick size depends on your container’s diameter, wax type, and fragrance load. Always test before finalizing your collection.
Browse our wick selection to find the right match.
Packaging is the finishing touch that turns candles into gifts. Options include:
Ready-made solutions: Standard boxes and bags you can personalize with labels.
Printing houses: Custom boxes with your branding.
Paper choice: Matte, kraft, or luxury laminated finishes can all affect perception.
Your packaging should reflect the identity of your collection and be practical for shipping.
Once your collection is created, the next step is sharing it with the world. A few ideas:
Photography: Invest time in beautiful photos that capture your candles’ mood.
Social media: Showcase the story behind your collection — why you chose these fragrances, colors, or jars.
Markets & events: A great way to get direct feedback from customers.
Seasonal launches: Tie your collection to a holiday or season to boost sale

Creating your first diffuser and room spray collection is an exciting journey. From the initial idea to the final product on the shelf, every choice — fragrance, base, reeds, bottles, packaging — builds the story of your brand.

Start with a clear vision, keep your line simple and cohesive, and focus on quality. Your customers will notice the care you put into your work, and your collection will quickly become a signature part of your offering.

blogs.title: Candle Fragrance Load Explained: How Much Oil Should You Use?
blogs.metaDescription: Understand candle fragrance load, how much oil to use, and how wax type, wick choice and process affect scent throw, burn and stability.
headings: Candle Fragrance Load Explained: How Much Oil Should You Use?
1. What is fragrance load (and why it matters)
2. How wax chemistry and wax type affect fragrance load
3. Step-by-step calculation and labeling of fragrance load
4. Practical process factors affecting how much fragrance oil you can safely use
5. Testing, curing and optimisation of fragrance load
6. Trouble-shooting: common issues when fragrance load is too high or too low
7. Commercial / regulatory considerations for fragrance load
8. Summary checklist for candle makers
Best Candle Waxes Compared: Soy vs Coconut vs Paraffin
Room Spray Guide: Ratios, Bases, and Fragrance Oils
textBlocks: Definition: Fragrance load is the ratio (by weight) of fragrance oil added to the candle wax. For example, if you have 100 g of wax and you add 8 g of fragrance oil, the fragrance load is 8%.
This is distinct from “fragrance content” (which sometimes is expressed as % of the finished candle weight).
The wax can only hold a certain amount of oil before structural or performance issues arise (bleeding, oil‐seepage, weak burn, excessive sooting).

Fragrance load affects both cold throw (the scent you smell when candle is unlit) and hot throw (when candle is burning). Too little oil = weak scent. Too much can degrade wick performance, melt pool control, and create safety issues.
Achieving a consistent load is key for repeatable produ ct quality, especially in small-batch or professional candle manufacturing.

Different waxes (soy, coconut, paraffin, blends) have differing capacities to hold fragrance oils, due to differences in molecular structure, melt point, viscosity, crystallisation behaviour, adhesion to containers, etc.

A guideline for soy waxes is generally of ~6-10% for optimal performance. Because soy wax tends to have a lower melt point and specific crystallisation behaviour, a high fragrance load may more easily cause migration or wet-spots in containers. Example: AAK “Golden Wax 464” (soy container wax) specifies a maximum fragrance load of around 10 %, with some suggestions starting at 6 % for best results.

Generally, other natural waxes like coconut or olive blends are considered higher perfume load capable ( up to 12%). Example: Kerax Olive™ Pillar Wax (olive + natural additives) states it can take up to 12 % fragrance load.

For paraffin and blends the capacity tends to vary, but many sources suggest 8-12% for certain blends. However, volume, burn performance and wick sizing may limit practical fragrance load even if technically possible.

There is no one-size-fits-all number. The maximum feasible fragrance load depends on: wax formulation, container size/shape, wick choice, fragrance oil properties (density, compatibility), curing time, pour temperature, etc. Always follow the wax manufacturer guidelines as starting point. At our website you can find those in the Properties table on each of the waxes pages.
Weight of fragrance oil, g = Total weight of the finished candle (wax + FO), g X (desired fragrance load, % : 100)
(Ensure you are using weights, not volumes)
Weight of wax, g = Total weight of the finished candle (wax + FO), g - Weight of fragrance oil, g
You plan to make a 200 g candle at 8% FO (of total weight)
F (Weight of fragrance oil, g) = 200 X (8:100) = 16 g
This version is easier for beginners to use:
Multiply by the desired % to get fragrance oil.
Always round weights to 1 decimal or nearest gram depending on batch size.
Keep a log of fragrance load, wax melted, pour temperature, wick size, cure time — to correlate with performance.
For multi-sku products: maintain a “standard fragrance load” (e.g., 8%) but test each fragrance oil individually (see section 5)
Even if your wax manufacturer says “up to 10%”, you may need to reduce fragrance load due to process/production factors. Below are critical variables:
Some fragrance oils are heavier, have higher flash points or include additives that don’t mix well with wax. Example: adding fragrance when wax is too hot can cause fragrance loss or migration.
If fragrance is “dense” (e.g., base-notes like patchouli/oud) it may depress wax melt point or increase sooting at higher load.
Pouring too cool or too hot influences adhesion, sink-holes, frosting, and may require reducing fragrance load. For example, AAK Golden Wax 464 (EU) suggests pour at 55-65 °C.
Slow cooling is beneficial for adhesion; rapid cooling may cause “wet spots” or separation if fragrance load is high.

A higher fragrance load increases volatility of hot throw but also increases demand on wick to deliver enough heat. If wick cannot support it, you may get tunnelling, incomplete melt pool or low scent throw.
Some sources suggest heavier waxes or higher fragrance loads require larger wick sizes.

Larger containers require stronger hot throw, which may mean higher fragrance load or more efficient wax/fragrance combination. But with larger volume, wax may melt more slowly and fragrance may be less efficiently diffused, so even if load is increased, effect may not scale linearly.

Fragrance dispersion and binding into wax matrix takes time; insufficient cure may make even correct fragrance load perform weakly. (See section 6) High fragrance load may show more bleeding or migration if cure is insufficient.
Colour dyes may absorb or react with fragrance oils, affecting throw.
Additives (e.g., stearic acid, wax boosters) may alter maximum safe fragrance load.
If you blend waxes (e.g., soy + paraffin), the safe fragrance load may change.
Select your wax and fragrance oil.
Determine a baseline fragrance load (e.g., 6% for soy).
Make small test batches at different loads (e.g., 6 %, 8 %, 10 %).
Use same container, same wick size, same pouring and curing conditions.
Measure cold throw (after solidified), hot throw (after burn pool establishment), visual finish, melt pool size, wick behaviour, any oil separation or wick clogging.
Note results and choose optimum load for that oil/wax combination.
For AAK 464 soy wax, one test indicated 2 weeks cure time for optimal scent throw.
For coconut/soy blends say at least 48 h but up to 1–2 weeks is beneficial for hot throw.
Insufficient curing may make your product underperform even if fragrance load is within spec.
On first burn, look for full melt pool, no tunnelling, flame stability.
Smell strength: cold throw in unlit environment, hot throw in standard sized room.
Surface appearance: no wet spots, frosting, or bloom (especially for soy wax)
Burn time: Ensure fragrance load hasn’t significantly reduced expected burn hours.
Customer feedback: Does the scent last / fade too quickly.
If performance at baseline is weak: increase fragrance load in small increments (e.g., +1 %) and retest.
If you reach manufacturer maximum load (e.g., 10 %) and still weak throw: consider switching wax or oil, or optimise wick / container size.
If you observe negative effects (sooting, oil seepage, wet spots): reduce fragrance load.
Weak cold throw and/or hot throw (customers comment “I can’t smell it”).
Candle may burn fine but lacks appeal.
Solution: incrementally increase fragrance load, retest; check that fragrance oil is quality and compatible.
Oily film on surface, wax separation or oil pooling on top of candle.
Sooting, large flame, erratic burn, wick carbon build-up, wax sucking down around wick.
Wet spots (especially in glass containers) or poor adhesion/detachment of wax from container.

Solution: reduce fragrance load; ensure correct wick size; check pour/cure parameters; ensure fragrance oil flash point is higher than pour/solidification temperature; test compatibility of fragrance oil with wax.
For commercial production, you must document fragrance load, testing results, burn time, safety data, component declarations.
For example, fragrance calculators provide weighting and regulatory guidance for CLP/IFRA compliance.
Pricing: fragrance oils are more expensive per gram than wax; using higher load increases cost per candle, so optimum may balance cost vs performance.
Labelling: Some jurisdictions require naming fragrance oils or stating “contains fragrance oil” and ensuring safe burn performance.
Batch records: It's advisable to keep records of wax batch, fragrance lot, load %, oven/ pour temperatures, cure time, burn test results for traceability and quality assurance.
Choose your wax and note manufacturer’s recommended fragrance load (and maximum).
Select initial fragrance load (start lower than max, e.g., soy = ~6 %).
Melt wax to recommended temperature, add fragrance oil at correct temp, stir thoroughly.
Pour at correct temperature into pre‐conditioned container.
Cure candles for recommended time (e.g., 48 h to 2 weeks depending on wax).
Test burn and evaluate: cold throw, hot throw, melt pool, surface finish, wick behaviour.
Adjust fragrance load upward or downward as needed based on test results.
Document batch metrics and ensure regulatory compliance.
For each new fragrance oil or wax lot, repeat testing – never assume results will match previous loads exactly.

blogs.title: Room Spray Guide: Ratios, Bases, and Fragrance Oils
blogs.metaDescription: Learn room spray ratios, fragrance load, base types and formula choices for clear, stable sprays with strong scent and reliable performance.
headings: Room Spray Guide: Ratios, Bases, and Fragrance Oils
1. What a room spray actually is (and what it is not)
2. Choosing your overall formulation strategy
When you design a formula, decide first:
3. Fragrance load: how much fragrance oil should you use?
Most professional suppliers suggest 3–10% fragrance oil in room sprays:
4. Bases: ethanol, solvent bases, and water systems
Practical starter formula (100 g / 100 ml batches)
5. Working with fragrance oils: compatibility, strength, and safety
6. Process and testing: getting reliable results
7. Safety, CLP/IFRA and VOC considerations
8. Troubleshooting: common room spray problems
9. Example development workflow for a new room spray
Best Candle Waxes Compared: Soy vs Coconut vs Paraffin
textBlocks: In formal terms, a room spray is a non-skin fragrance product designed to be sprayed into the air or onto selected textiles and surfaces. Regulatory frameworks usually place room sprays in IFRA Category 9 (air fresheners and some fabric sprays), which determines the maximum allowable fragrance percentage for each specific fragrance.
A basic room spray has four functional components:
Fragrance oils, essential oils, or blends.
Solvent base (e.g. Augeo® Clean Multi or commercial “room spray base”),
Humectant or other actives (for linen/pillow mists).
There are three main strategies that serious makers use:
1. Alcohol-based sprays (classic fine mist, fast evaporation)
Carrier: high-proof ethanol or commercial alcohol room-spray base.
Pros: clear solutions, quick-drying, strong initial impact, low residue.
Cons: flammable, more regulatory constraints for transport and storage.
2. Solvent-base sprays (e.g. eco-solvent like Augeo Clean Multi)
Carrier: specialty solvents optimised for home fragrance.
Pros: clear, non-flammable (for some bases), very good solubility, strong scent, longer linger.
Cons: can feel “oilier” on some surfaces, not always suitable for skin.
3. Water-based sprays with solubiliser (often for linens & fabric)
Carrier: demineralised water + solubiliser (e.g. Polysorbate-20) + a small alcohol fraction.
Pros: less flammable, softer profile for textiles, easier perception as “natural”.
Cons: needs preservative, can be hazy/milky, may leave a slight residue if overused.
Target: air only, air + textiles, or multi-surface.
Brand position: eco/solvent-free, alcohol-style fine fragrance, or high-impact industrial.
Legal constraints: IFRA limits, CLP/GHS labeling (EU/UK), VOC regulations (e.g. CARB in the US).
3–5% for subtle mists, delicate or strong oils.
8–10% for stronger impact, provided the IFRA limit for that fragrance allows it.

For certain solvent bases like Augeo® Clean Multi, some diffuser formulas use up to 15–25% fragrance, mainly for reed diffusers.For room sprays, you usually don’t need to go that high; 3–10% is normally plenty.

Each fragrance oil comes with an IFRA certificate listing the maximum allowed percentage for each category (Category 9 = air fresheners, some fabric sprays). Professional guides strongly recommend respecting these limits when choosing your load.
If your target is 8% but IFRA Category 9 max is 3%, you must stay at or below 3%.
Several pro recipes for room/linen sprays use a small % of alcohol + water + solubiliser, but in a pure alcohol system you can keep it very simple: alcohol + fragrance.
Very fast evaporation → crisp, non-sticky finish.
Generally clear if your fragrance is compatible.
Some customers dislike the ethanol odour on spray (usually flashes off in seconds).
Target: Air room spray (no direct fabric use on delicate textiles).
Weigh fragrance oil into a beaker.
Add perfumer’s alcohol, stir until fully clear.
Let rest 24–48 h (or more) to allow micro-bubbles to escape and the scent to “marry”.
Filter if needed, then bottle in fine-mist atomisers.
For stronger scent, go up to 8–10% fragrance if allowed by IFRA Category 9 and flashpoint considerations.
For a softer linen spray, reduce to 3–4%, and test on fabrics.
4.2 Eco-solvent bases (Augeo® and commercial room spray bases)
Augeo Clean Multi is a bio-based solvent widely used in reed diffusers and room/linen sprays. It has:
Very good solubilising power for fragrance oils.
VOC-compliant status in several jurisdictions (e.g. CARB-compatible scent loading).

For room sprays, 3–10% fragrance in an Augeo-type base is usually sufficient. Many “eco room spray bases” from candle suppliers are built on similar logic: bio solvent + modifiers, designed for 3–10% fragrance.
Excellent clarity and stability with fragrance.
Often non-hazardous or low-hazard, sometimes not requiring CLP label at low loads.
Good long-lasting scent and slow, even evaporation.
Feels slightly less “dry” than pure alcohol on textiles.
Still requires CLP/IFRA assessment at higher fragrance loads.
Target: Room & light textile spray with non-flammable base.
93% eco room-spray base or Augeo Clean Multi (93 g)
Add to the eco base (Augeo or similar), stir slowly until the blend goes homogeneous and clear.
Rest 24–72 h to check for separation or haziness.
Bottle in trigger sprays or fine misters.
You can scale fragrance between 3-10%; for home fragrance, higher loads (15–20%) are typical for diffusers, not necessary for sprays.
Always re-check CLP classification when you change the percentage.
Optional preservative (e.g. sodium benzoate) when water is present.
Pros: Lower alcohol content.Softer, textile-friendly positioning (depending on fragrance).

Watchpoints: Some perfumers note that large water fractions increase solubility problems; many fragrance ingredients are not water-soluble, and solubiliser residues can feelsticky or slipperyon surfaces and floors.Always test on fabrics to avoid staining or residue.
Target: Linen & room mist with reduced alcohol. Many suppliers recommend 1:2:4 Preservative: Polysorbate-20: Fragrance oil by weight to obtain a clear or nearly clear solution.

First, you need to decide what percentage of fragrance oil you want to add — usually between 3–10%. Then, determine the total weight of your system, which is usually equal to the volume of your bottle.
Mix fragrance oil and Polysorbate-20; stir until the blend becomes uniform and as clear as possible.
Add preservative according to supplier instructions.
Expect a slightly milky appearance; this is normal for many Polysorbate systems and is often hidden by opaque or frosted bottles.
Always perform stability tests and a fabric test. (The test should be performed on white fabric)
Get the IFRA certificate from your supplier.
Find the limit for Category 9 (room sprays, air fresheners).
Make sure your chosen % is at or below that limit.
Some fragrance oils contain highly polar ingredients and emulsifiers → easier to solubilise.
Others are rich in heavy base notes (e.g. resins, ouds, patchouli) → harder to solubilise, more likely to haze.
Try a different solubiliser or fragrance.
Try a different eco-base or fragrance family.
Add slowly while stirring gently to avoid excessive foaming.
Add in several portions, mixing thoroughly.
Add at the end, according to supplier guidelines.
What else is important? Slow mixing, resting time and small pilot batches before scaling up.
Before you consider a formula “final”, run basic tests:
24–72 h clarity check at room temperature.
Freeze–thaw test:24 h in the fridge/freezer, then back to room temperature. Observe for separation, sediment, or permanent haziness.
Shake test: Shake vigorously, leave to stand. Persistent foam after several hours can annoy end-users.
Test in a realistic room size:
1–2 sprays at standard distance (30–50 cm from air or textiles).
Spray from a distance (30–50 cm) on white cotton swatch.

For the EU/UK, if your product is classified as hazardous (e.g. due to fragrance hazards), you may need a CLP label. For example, guides for working with Augeo + fragrance (up to 25%) show how hazard classifications change with load and provide label templates.

Some commercial room-spray bases and linen bases are formulated to be non-hazardous at typical fragrance loads, meaning they may not require a CLP label by themselves, but you must always reassess after adding fragrance.
Room sprays typically fall under IFRA Category 9:
The IFRA limit for each fragrance (Category 9) is the absolute ceiling for your fragrance % in the formula.
If you sell internationally, keep IFRA-compliant documentation (Formula, IFRA certs, SDS) in your batch files.

Alcohol-based sprays are flammable and subject to transport rules (ADR, IATA). Eco-solvent bases such as Augeo Clean Multi are designed as low VOC, non-flammable options that comply with VOC regulations (e.g. CARB) while still allowing relatively high fragrance loads in air-care products.
Insufficient solubiliser for the fragrance load.
Too much water in a system with lipophilic ingredients.
Switch to a different base (e.g. eco-solvent) or different fragrance.
Incorrect mixing order or insufficient mixing time.
Pre-mix fragrance + solubiliser thoroughly before adding water or main base.
Allow a longer rest period and retest.
If separation persists after 48–72 h, adjust ratios or change solubiliser/base.
Perfumers note that water-heavy sprays with non-volatile solubilisers can leave sticky/slippery residues on surfaces, as the solubiliser stays behind when the water evaporates.
Use more volatile carriers (ethanol or eco-solvent).
Adjust instructions (“spray into the air, not directly onto floors”).
Fragrance itself has low diffusion in air.
Increase fragrance load within IFRA limits (e.g. from 3% → 5% → 7%).
Switch to more diffusive fragrance families (citrus, aromatics, aldehydic notes).
Target: “Citrus–herbal kitchen spray, strong but not overpowering, alcohol-based.”
Check IFRA Category 9 limit for the chosen fragrance
Check clarity, no separation, no sediment.
Do performance tests in a real room.
If Trial A is too weak and Trial B is perfect, you keep 7%.
If both are harsh, drop to 4–5% and/or adjust fragrance choice (softer profile).
Keep record: weights, temperatures, mixing steps, observations.
Calculate hazards for the chosen % using supplier data and any CLP calculators for your base system.

blogs.title: Customer Retention in Home Fragrance: Why Clients Come Back
blogs.metaDescription: Learn why clients return to candle and home fragrance brands: scent performance, repeat purchase metrics, reorders and retention strategy.
headings: Customer Retention in Home Fragrance: Why Clients Come Back
Best Candle Waxes Compared: Soy vs Coconut vs Paraffin
Room Spray Guide: Ratios, Bases, and Fragrance Oils
textBlocks: For a candle, reed diffuser or room spray brand, customer retention is simple to define and hard to achieve: it’s the share of people who buy from you again after their first purchase. In home fragrance, that second purchase almost never happens by accident. It only happens if the product performs beautifully in a real home, not just on the shelf.

A loyal customer is someone who finishes your candle and deliberately looks for your brand again — online, in a flower shop or in a décor store. That loyalty is built on one core thing: fragrance performance.

Beautiful jars, diffuser bottles and labels are essential. In home decor stores, gift or flower shops, attractive packaging plus a nice display works perfectly for impulse purchases: a customer sees the candle, likes the look, smells it quickly — and buys.
But if, at home, the scent is
shallow and boring, they will not become a repeat buyer.
Design sells the first unit. The fragrance sells the second, third and tenth.
Your customer doesn’t know the terms “cold throw” and “hot throw”, but they feel them every time.
Cold throw – how strongly the product smells unlit or at rest (candle on a shelf, closed diffuser, first spray in the air).
Hot throw / active diffusion – how the scent fills the room when the candle burns, the reeds are fully saturated, or the spray has settled on textiles and surfaces.
the scent is clearly noticeable, not just a hint,
it reaches beyond the immediate area of the product, and
it stays present for a reasonable time.
This applies equally to candles, diffusers and room sprays. Different bases — same expectation: the room should actually smell of something pleasant.
High-quality fragrance oils are engineered to deliver strong, stable scent throw and to work predictably with waxes, bases and reeds. You’re not just buying a “nice smell”; you’re buying a structure.
Multi-layered – clear top, heart and base notes that shift over time.
Natural-feeling – no plastic, no harsh “cheap air freshener” effect.
Consistent – same character and strength from batch to batch.

The more carefully these notes are built, the more “expensive” and interesting the scent feels in a room. Thin, low-grade oils often smell harsh or disappear quickly. Your customer may not know why, but they feel that something is “off” — and they quietly switch to another brand.

If you sell through furniture stores, interior boutiques or luxury gift shops, you usually never meet the final customer. The decision to buy — and to buy again — is made entirely based on the product itself.
In this model your product must:
Look premium – clean wax, tidy labels, elegant bottles.
Give a clear first impression – strong but not aggressive cold throw on the shelf.
Work reliably at home – good hot throw for candles, stable diffusion for reed diffusers, lasting presence for room sprays.
Stay consistent – same scent profile and strength every time the shop reorders.
If the product disappoints, you lose both the end customer and the shop’s trust.
You don’t need a complex CRM to understand your retention. Start with simple, candle-specific metrics:
Formula (per month or quarter): Returning customers ÷ All customers who bought in that period
Example: 30 repeat buyers out of 120 total buyers → 25% repeat purchase rate.
Share of orders from returning customers (online)
Look at your webshop stats: Orders from returning customers ÷ All orders
If this grows over time, your product and experience are working.
Track how often each shop reorders the same fragrance or collection.
If they only try you once and never reorder that scent, there’s a product or performance issue.
Pick one month of new customers and see how many of them buy again within 3–6 months.
Do this a few times a year to see if you’re improving.
Keep these numbers in a simple spreadsheet. Retention doesn’t have to be perfect, but it should be visible and steadily going up as you improve the product.
6. What to Do with Your Retention Data
Once you track these numbers, you can use them:
Upgrade or kill weak scents.If one fragrance always appears in first orders but rarely in repeat orders, the scent is probably not good enough. Reformulate or discontinue.
Strengthen your bestsellers.Scents that appear again and again in reorders are your core line. Protect them: secure supply of oils, standardize recipes, and make sure they are always in stock.

Adjust your channel strategy.If retention is strong online but weak in certain shops, the issue might be display, wrong audience, or poor explanation from staff. Work with the retailer or move those scents elsewhere.
Measure the impact of any change.Change wax, wicks or fragrance supplier? Watch retention and complaints for 1–2 cycles. If repeat orders drop, the “improvement” wasn’t an improvement.
Retention is not only about fragrance quality. It’s also about how you show loyalty to your customer so they feel loyal to you.
Concrete ideas for a candle/diffuser/room-spray brand:
Make it easy to come back.
Put clear scent names and burn/diffusion notes on labels.
Include a small “scent card” or postcard in the box so people remember what they bought and can find it again.
Simple loyalty codes: “10% off on your second order of the same scent”.
Small free tealight or mini wax melt in a new fragrance with every second or third order.
Take responsibility when something goes wrong.
If the customer says “I can’t smell it” or “the candle tunnels badly”, offer a replacement, a discount on the next order, or a personal troubleshooting guide.
One generous solution is cheaper than losing a customer forever.
Note what each wholesale client sells best: “They love woods and ambers; they don’t move gourmands.”
For direct customers, segment email lists by preference: fresh, floral, gourmand, woody, etc., and recommend new scents accordingly.
Share behind-the-scenes: how you test scents, why you choose certain oils, how you reject weak formulas.
Let loyal clients test new fragrances first and ask for their feedback. People are more loyal to what they helped create.
Customer retention in home fragrance products is built on one main question:“Would I happily live with this scent again and again?”
fills the space with a deep, multi-layered, natural-smelling fragrance, and
is backed by a brand that treats customers fairly and personally,
then your buyers will return exactly for that feeling — an interesting, long-lasting and authentic scent in their home.

textBlocks: This article is for beginner candle makers who want to understand why interior candles are needed, what formats to produce, how they should look, how to price them, and who actually buys them.
For a professional interior designer, a candle is not “just a smell”. A good interior candle adds three things at once:

Light – a soft, warm, moving light instead of a cold overhead lamp. Form – a decorative object that completes a coffee table, shelf or mantelpiece. Scent – a mood for the space: calm, focus, “home feeling”.
Light – a soft, warm, moving light instead of a cold overhead lamp.
Form – a decorative object that completes a coffee table, shelf or mantelpiece.
Scent – a mood for the space: calm, focus, “home feeling”.
Candles are one of the easiest ways to:
create a cosy evening atmosphere, highlight key areas in a room, make a space emotionally memorable.
highlight key areas in a room,
Clusters of container and pillar candles on a coffee table, console or fireplace. Combined with books, trays, vases and small objects.
Clusters of container and pillar candles on a coffee table, console or fireplace.
Combined with books, trays, vases and small objects.
Taper candles on the table for everyday and festive settings. Subtle scented candles that don’t fight with the smell of food.
Taper candles on the table for everyday and festive settings.
Subtle scented candles that don’t fight with the smell of food.
One or two softly scented candles on a bedside table or dresser. Calm, dim light for winding down.
One or two softly scented candles on a bedside table or dresser.
Calm, dim light for winding down.
“Home spa”: candles around the bath, on shelves and ledges.
One neat candle on a desk or shelf to define a “small island” of comfort and focus.
Understanding these real use cases is crucial: you’re not making “abstract products”, you’re creating tools for these situations.
When you think in “interior” terms, your audience becomes wider and more professional.
through your website, marketplaces, Instagram, at markets and fairs, for themselves and as gifts.
through your website, marketplaces, Instagram, at markets and fairs,

clear photos “in the interior”, simple language: “for living room”, “for bedroom”, “for bathroom”, a price that feels like a good alternative to well-known brands (not a suspiciously cheap product, but not unreachable luxury).
simple language: “for living room”, “for bedroom”, “for bathroom”,
a price that feels like a good alternative to well-known brands (not a suspiciously cheap product, but not unreachable luxury).
For designers the candle is a tool, not just a nice object. What they care about:
stable quality, predictable scents (client shouldn’t complain), neutral, flexible colour palette, good wholesale conditions (often around 50% of retail with a reasonable minimum order).
good wholesale conditions (often around 50% of retail with a reasonable minimum order).
A practical step:Create a PDF catalogue for designers with:
interior photos, sizes, colours, scents, wholesale prices and minimum order quantities.
wholesale prices and minimum order quantities.

a clear, compact range for their shelves (for example: 3–4 scents, 2 sizes of container candles, a few pillar/taper options), packaging that sells visually, enough margin between your wholesale and their retail.
a clear, compact range for their shelves (for example: 3–4 scents, 2 sizes of container candles, a few pillar/taper options),
enough margin between your wholesale and their retail.
taper and pillar candles for tables and public areas, subtle scents that don’t overpower food or existing ambient smell.
taper and pillar candles for tables and public areas,
subtle scents that don’t overpower food or existing ambient smell.
Corporate gifting, developers, event agencies love:
sets of 2–3 candles, custom packaging, logo labels, branded cards.
custom packaging, logo labels, branded cards.
This segment values reliability and flexibility more than romantic stories.
Designers and shops love container candles because they are:
safer (wax is inside a vessel), visually neat (even when the candle is not burning), easy to place in almost any style of interior.
visually neat (even when the candle is not burning),
easy to place in almost any style of interior.

90–120 ml – small size: testers, bathrooms, bedside tables. 180–230 ml – main “working” candle for living rooms and bedrooms. 300+ ml – larger interior piece, can be a central object in a composition.
90–120 ml – small size: testers, bathrooms, bedside tables.
180–230 ml – main “working” candle for living rooms and bedrooms.
300+ ml – larger interior piece, can be a central object in a composition.
matte or satin glass, solid ceramics, concrete/stone for more brutal or minimal interiors.
concrete/stone for more brutal or minimal interiors.
simple cylinder or tumbler, low, wide bowls (usually with 3–4 wicks).
Pillar and sculptural candles are used:
on fireplaces, on console tables, in seasonal and festive arrangements (Christmas, autumn, weddings).
in seasonal and festive arrangements (Christmas, autumn, weddings).

2–3 diameters and 2–3 heights that combine well in groups. Colour palette: neutral base (ivory, warm white, greige, grey, black) + a few seasonal accent colours. Sculptural candles: focus more on shape and surface; scent should be light or none at all – they must look good even unlit.
2–3 diameters and 2–3 heights that combine well in groups.
Colour palette: neutral base (ivory, warm white, greige, grey, black) + a few seasonal accent colours.
Sculptural candles: focus more on shape and surface; scent should be light or none at all – they must look good even unlit.
table settings, restaurants, weddings and events.
length around 25–30 cm, sold in sets (2, 4 or 6 pieces), usually unscented or with a very delicate scent – they shouldn’t interfere with food aromas.
usually unscented or with a very delicate scent – they shouldn’t interfere with food aromas.
It often makes sense to have two parallel lines:
Interior scented candles – for light + fragrance (mostly containers). Purely decorative candles – for form and colour (pillar, sculptural, taper), often unscented.
Interior scented candles – for light + fragrance (mostly containers).
Purely decorative candles – for form and colour (pillar, sculptural, taper), often unscented.
This allows you to serve both needs: atmosphere and visual styling.
Think of scent like another layer of design. Different rooms need different functions. Minimal “starter” fragrance map for a home:

Living room – welcoming and warm: woods, resins, soft spices, a touch of gourmand (vanilla, tonka, tobacco accord). Bedroom – relaxation: lavender, iris, soft florals, woods, musks. Kitchen/dining area – compatible with food: citrus, green notes, herbs, light florals; clean and transparent compositions. Bathroom/spa area: eucalyptus, mint, marine notes, clean “spa” accords. Home office/library: woods, leather, smoky, inky or “library” notes.
Living room – welcoming and warm: woods, resins, soft spices, a touch of gourmand (vanilla, tonka, tobacco accord).
Bedroom – relaxation: lavender, iris, soft florals, woods, musks.
Kitchen/dining area – compatible with food: citrus, green notes, herbs, light florals; clean and transparent compositions.
Bathroom/spa area: eucalyptus, mint, marine notes, clean “spa” accords.
Home office/library: woods, leather, smoky, inky or “library” notes.
For a beginner, 4–6 well-structured scents are much better than a random catalogue of 30 oils. You’re building a system, not a chaos of smells.
Interior-focused brands bet on shape, texture and palette, not on loud decoration.
simple, clean forms, matte / satin finishes, tactile materials: ceramics, concrete, thick glass.
tactile materials: ceramics, concrete, thick glass.
base: white, ivory, greige, grey, black; 1–2 accent colour lines per season (for example: olive, terracotta, deep blue).
base: white, ivory, greige, grey, black;
1–2 accent colour lines per season (for example: olive, terracotta, deep blue).
readable typography, neat logo placement, minimum visual noise – the candle must not look cheap or “too busy” in a refined interior.
minimum visual noise – the candle must not look cheap or “too busy” in a refined interior.
Your goal: a candle that a designer will gladly put into a finished project, not hide in a cupboard.
For each candle format, calculate full cost per unit:

wax, fragrance, wick, vessel, lid, labels, box, your labour (how many candles you realistically produce per hour and how much your hour is worth), a fair share of overheads (rent, equipment, tests, marketing, website, taxes).

This gives you COGS per candle (for example, 5 €).

Example:If COGS of a 200 ml interior candle is about 5 €:
wholesale price – around 10 €, retail price – around 18–22 € (depending on positioning and market).
retail price – around 18–22 € (depending on positioning and market).
This range is typical for the “good middle/accessible premium” segment.

For stronger branding, more expensive vessels and packaging, the same 200 ml size can easily be sold for 22–28 € or more. Larger multi-wick candles in heavy glass or ceramics can be in the 60–120+ € bracket – this is normal for the premium interior segment.
COGS often around 4–6 €, wholesale – roughly 9–12 €, retail – roughly 18–28 €.

Range: 4 scents in 200 ml containers (COGS around 5 €).
Positioning: “interior soy candles”, modern, cosy, not super-luxury but clearly not mass supermarket level.
Channels: online shop, Instagram, local markets.
Result: margin is sufficient to cover materials, testing and basic marketing.
Range: neutral collection in matte glass 30 cl, 3–4 calm scents, wooden lid, box.
Plus: one exclusive colour that matches the showroom palette (can be priced slightly higher).
Range: large 3-wick candles 400–600 ml in heavy ceramics or thick glass, limited palette, complex scents.
COGS: easily 12–20+ € per candle (expensive vessel, a lot of wax, manual work).
Retail price: 60–120+ €, positioned as a decorative object, not just a candle.
Such pieces are closer to designer home decor than to “just home fragrance”.
Interior candles are tools of design: light + form + scent, not just something that “smells nice”.
Focus on the formats that designers actually use:
Build a structured scent line for different zones of the home instead of a random list of oils.
Make your candles look calm, tactile and interior-friendly – they must feel natural in a finished project.
Set prices from real COGS and clear positioning, aiming at 50–65% gross margin, instead of “what feels ok today”.
Remember that your clients are not only private buyers, but also designers, shops, HoReCa and gifting services – each of them has their own needs.
